title: STUDY TOPIC: Nativity part 7
scripture_text: ‘’This is how the birth of Jesus the Messiah came about. His mother Mary was pledged to be married to Joseph, but before they came together, she was found to be pregnant through the Holy Spirit. Because Joseph her husband was faithful to the law, and yet did not want to expose her to public disgrace, he had in mind to divorce her quietly but after he had considered this, an angel of the Lord appeared to him in a dream and said, “Joseph son of David, do not be afraid to take Mary home as your wife, because what is conceived in her is from the Holy Spirit. She will give birth to a son, and you are to give him the name Jesus, because he will save his people from their sins.” All this took place to fulfil what the Lord had said through the prophet: “The virgin will conceive and give birth to a son, and they will call him Immanuel” (which means “God with us”). When Joseph woke up, he did what the angel of the Lord had commanded him and took Mary home as his wife. But he did not consummate their marriage until she gave birth to a son. And he gave him the name Jesus.’’
devotional:
Joseph needed to create space so God could work in Mary’s life.

God was asking Joseph to make choices that would enhance and encourage what God had already began to do in Mary… Matthew 1:25 says But he did not consummate their marriage until she gave birth to a son. At surface level this verse is telling us that Joseph and Mary were not physically intimate until after Jesus was born. However, in this verse there is a very important principle that is an essential for every Christian marriage. There will be times when we will have to sacrifice our desires, wants, ‘needs’ so that God can complete a work he is doing in the life of your spouse. This principle could apply to many areas of life.

Marriage should never be tit-for-tat.

Do we hold our spouse accountable for moral, upright behaviour? Yes!

Do we withhold our love when we feel we are not being loved? No!

Christ’s invitation to love is without limits, without strings attached. We are called to love freely and sacrifice for our spouse sincerely.

Are we prepared to make substantial and continuous sacrifice so God can produce the life of Jesus in your spouse?

I wonder what kind of concern or care do we have for the spiritual lives of those we are married to. I totally realise that we will never be held responsible for another persons spirituality, but we have a God given responsibility to encourage and help others to grow and mature spiritually, especially those we love most.

For Joseph and Mary the very centre of their marriage was the activity of God and the life of Jesus, and as Christians we should endeavour to have the same in our homes and marriages. But how can we do that?

Here are a few suggestions.

a. Pray about each others spiritual life and their relationship with God, remember your spouses relationship with God is the most important thing in their lives.

b. Pray together, not as some kind of formal activity, but spontaneously, for example, if you’re discussing something, or making plans, make prayer a part of the discussion.

c. Be intentional in wanting to encourage your spouse spiritually, make it a deliberate choice.

d. Explore the word of God together, talk about what you hear in church, ask questions of each other, share verses,

e. Offer assistance. Just ask is there anything I can do to help you? Take on practical task to free the other person up so they can go and pray, or read, or do some kind of ministry.

f. Plan spiritual activities together. It could a morning praying together, or going on a retreat with friends, or it just might something like promising to pray for each other during a difficult day when you are apart.

g. Be prepared to make sacrifices and go out of your way to encourage others spiritually.

Joseph displayed a heart like Jesus. When you look at the life of Joseph you get the impression that he was the kind of man who did not care much for the limelight, he didn’t write any books, preach any sermons, do any miracles. We are never told if he was especially gifted, or if he had accomplished great things. All we know is that he was a quiet godly man who lived in the obscurity of Nazareth. Bringing up the Son of God in Nazareth was like the queen being brought up in an unknown and undesirable place. Nazareth was a small unimpressive place with a bad reputation. Joseph choose to live there, see Matthew 2:23

Joseph, like Jesus did not live out of an ego. He knew that he could make a difference by living a godly life. We are told about Jesus that ‘although he was rich, yet for our sakes he became poor.’ 2 Corinthians 8:9.

Joseph embodied this same kind of attitude. Being a father to the Son of God could have caused him to look for notoriety, or popularity, but instead he embraced the obscurity of Nazareth. I think there is something very beautiful about humility in the home. Humility in the home makes the home a better place.

Humility is found in many forms such as modesty, meekness, humbleness, lowliness of mind, and submission. These are all things that go against our natural desire to excel and be seen in this world. We are often driven by a need to be right, to be in charge and to come first, and time and again, we deceive ourselves into thinking that settling for anything less is a sign of weakness. Writing about pride C.S. Lewis wrote: - ‘According to Christian teachers, the essential vice, the utmost evil, is Pride. Unchastity, anger, greed, drunkenness, and all that, are mere flea bites in comparison: it was through Pride that the devil became the devil: Pride leads to every other vice: it is the complete anti-God state of mind…… it is Pride which has been the chief cause of misery in every nation and every family since the world began.’ Source unknown.

I think the most important lesson we can learn from Joseph is that life is essentially about character, not ability, or achievement, or position or even opportunity, but about who we are and what we are becoming. Every thing Joseph was, we can be. We have all the opportunity to build families were God feels loved. We don’t need to be rich or clever, or strong, or loud, we don’t need to be specially gifted, or be well connected. But, you do need to have a humble love for righteousness and a heart that is prepared to make any sacrifice necessary so God can complete his work in the life of your spouse. We should aim to become families where God feels loved.
prayer: Father may our homes be places were we display encouraging humble love.
html:
STUDY TOPIC: Nativity part 7

Study Text: Matthew 1:18-25
‘’This is how the birth of Jesus the Messiah came about. His mother Mary was pledged to be married to Joseph, but before they came together, she was found to be pregnant through the Holy Spirit. Because Joseph her husband was faithful to the law, and yet did not want to expose her to public disgrace, he had in mind to divorce her quietly but after he had considered this, an angel of the Lord appeared to him in a dream and said, “Joseph son of David, do not be afraid to take Mary home as your wife, because what is conceived in her is from the Holy Spirit. She will give birth to a son, and you are to give him the name Jesus, because he will save his people from their sins.” All this took place to fulfil what the Lord had said through the prophet: “The virgin will conceive and give birth to a son, and they will call him Immanuel” (which means “God with us”). When Joseph woke up, he did what the angel of the Lord had commanded him and took Mary home as his wife. But he did not consummate their marriage until she gave birth to a son. And he gave him the name Jesus.’’

Joseph needed to create space so God could work in Mary’s life.

g. Be prepared to make sacrifices and go out of your way to encourage others spiritually.

Joseph displayed a heart like Jesus. When you look at the life of Joseph you get the impression that he was the kind of man who did not care much for the limelight, he didn’t write any books, preach any sermons, do any miracles. Joseph embodied this same kind of attitude. Being a father to the Son of God could have caused him to look for notoriety, or popularity, but instead he embraced the obscurity of Nazareth. I think there is something very beautiful about humility in the home. Humility in the home makes the home a better place.

Humility is found in many forms such as modesty, meekness, humbleness, lowliness of mind, and submission. These are all things that go against our natural desire to excel and be seen in this world. We are often driven by a need to be right, to be in charge and to come first, and time and again, we deceive ourselves into thinking that settling for anything less is a sign of weakness. Writing about pride C.S. Lewis wrote: - ‘According to Christian teachers, the essential vice, the utmost evil, is Pride. Unchastity, anger, greed, drunkenness, and all that, are mere flea bites in comparison: it was through Pride that the devil became the devil: Pride leads to every other vice: it is the complete anti-God state of mind…… it is Pride which has been the chief cause of misery in every nation and every family since the world began.’ Source unknown.

I think the most important lesson we can learn from Joseph is that life is essentially about character, not ability, or achievement, or position or even opportunity, but about who we are and what we are becoming. Every thing Joseph was, we can be. We have all the opportunity to build families were God feels loved. We don’t need to be rich or clever, or strong, or loud, we don’t need to be specially gifted, or be well connected. But, you do need to have a humble love for righteousness and a heart that is prepared to make any sacrifice necessary so God can complete his work in the life of your spouse. We should aim to become families where God feels loved.

Prayer: Father may our homes be places were we display encouraging humble love.
Bible in a year reading plan: - Ezra 4-7
